metadata.summary: Functional component patterns using React's built-in hooks — state, effects, memoization, refs, reducers, context, and reusable custom hook extraction.

content.title: React Hooks
content.summary: Functional component patterns using React's built-in hooks — state, effects, memoization, refs, reducers, context, and reusable custom hook extraction.

why.p: Hooks are the only state/lifecycle mechanism in modern React. Misusing them (stale closures, missing cleanup, over-memoization, conditional calls) causes subtle bugs that are hard to diagnose.

list.item: All functional components needing local state or side effects
Extracting reusable stateful logic into custom hooks
Performance optimization via memoization (with profiling first)
Sharing state across a component tree via Context
Class components (use the React docs migration path)
Logic that belongs in a state manager (Zustand, TanStack Query) — don't replicate server cache or global UI state with local hooks

rule.statement: Update object state immutably via functional updater; use lazy initializer for expensive computations; prefer useReducer when state shape grows beyond 3 fields.
rule.rationale: Direct mutation does not trigger re-render; functional updater avoids stale-closure bugs; lazy initializer runs only on mount.

example.title: useState Patterns

rule.statement: Use useRef for DOM references, for mutable values that must not trigger re-renders, and for stable callback references; never read .current during render.
rule.rationale: Reading .current during render makes the component non-deterministic; useRef mutations are synchronous but outside React's reconciliation cycle.

example.title: useRef Patterns

antipattern.description: Reading ref.current inside JSX or during the render function body.
antipattern.reason: The value may differ between React's two rendering passes (Concurrent Mode), causing visual inconsistencies.

rule.statement: Always return a cleanup function from effects that subscribe to external resources; never define the async function inline — define it inside and call it.
rule.rationale: Without cleanup, effects accumulate subscriptions on every render cycle, causing memory leaks. Async inline effects cannot return a cleanup because async functions return a Promise, not a function.

example.title: Effect Cleanup
Data Fetching with AbortController

rule.statement: Use a callbackRef to attach event listeners with an empty dependency array when the handler needs access to the latest props or state without re-registering on every change.
rule.rationale: Re-registering event listeners on every render is wasteful; the ref always holds the latest value without being a dependency.

example.title: Stable Event Listener Pattern

antipattern.description: Omitting a variable from the dependency array to avoid re-running the effect — fixing with a functional state updater instead.
antipattern.reason: Stale closure: the effect captures an old value of count. Use the functional updater form to read the current value without declaring it as a dependency.

example.title: Stale Closure Antipattern
Using Functional Updater

antipattern.description: Passing an object or array literal as a dependency — it creates a new reference every render, causing the effect to re-run continuously.
antipattern.reason: Object identity comparison: {} !== {} even with equal contents. Use primitive deps or memoize the object with useMemo.

example.title: Object Dependency Antipattern
example.code: // BAD — options is a new object every render useEffect(() => { fetchData(options); }, [options]);

example.title: Primitive Dependencies
example.code: // GOOD — primitive deps useEffect(() => { fetchData({ page, limit }); }, [page, limit]);

rule.statement: Apply useCallback only to functions passed as props to memoized children or as effect dependencies; apply useMemo only to computations that are demonstrably slow under profiling or that stabilize object references for effect deps.
rule.rationale: Both hooks add overhead (extra memory + comparison on every render). Premature memoization makes code harder to read without measurable benefit.

example.title: useCallback and useMemo

antipattern.reason: The memoization overhead exceeds the cost of the computation; just calculate inline.

example.code: const doubled = count * 2; // just compute it

rule.statement: Prefer useReducer over multiple useState calls when state fields change together or when the next state depends on the previous state across multiple transitions.
rule.rationale: A reducer co-locates all state transitions in one function, making it easier to test and reason about, and eliminates race conditions from interleaved setState calls.

example.title: useReducer for Complex State

section.name: Custom Hooks and Context Provider Pattern

rule.statement: Name custom hooks with the use prefix; return a typed interface (not a tuple unless it mirrors useState); declare the return interface explicitly above the hook.
rule.rationale: A named interface makes the hook's contract visible at the call site and prevents callers from relying on positional tuple indices that break on refactoring.

example.title: Custom Hook Shape
useDebounce Hook

rule.statement: Initialize Context with null and throw a descriptive error in the consumer hook when the value is null; wrap the mutable value in useMemo so consumers do not re-render on every provider render.
rule.rationale: A null sentinel makes it impossible to consume context outside its provider without a silent bug. Memoizing the value object prevents all consumers from re-rendering when an unrelated piece of provider state changes.

example.title: Context Provider with Hook

antipattern.description: Calling hooks conditionally based on a prop or runtime flag.
antipattern.reason: React relies on a stable hook call order across renders. Conditional hooks violate the Rules of Hooks and cause runtime errors.
